    YEAR 6 MATHEMATICS

    Topic 6: Space
    Year 6 KSSR

    Angles, regular polygons, interior angles, and geometry problem solving.

    Wong Jenn Jia (JJ) — Mathematics Educator · Tech Enthusiast · Instructional Designer

    Introduction 6.1 Angles 6.2 Regular Polygons 6.2.2 Interior Angles 6.3 Problem Solving Summary
    “
    Geometry helps students see that mathematics is not abstract only on paper,
    but visible in every corner, edge, and shape around them.
    1 Introduction

    Welcome to the World of Space

    Year 6 KSSR geometry focuses on how shapes, angles, and measurement describe the space around us, from clocks and floor tiles to solid objects.

    🚀 Geometry Is Everywhere
    • "Space" in mathematics is about shapes, lines, angles, and how they fit together.
    • We use geometry to describe corners, patterns, boundaries, and solid objects in daily life.
    • This topic builds practical drawing, measuring, and problem-solving skills.
    Space is easier to learn when you can picture it

    Students usually understand angles and polygons faster when they connect them to familiar objects like clocks, tiles, signs, and boxes.

    2 6.1 Angles

    The Art of the Turn

    📐 What Is an Angle
    • An angle is formed when two straight lines meet.
    • The common endpoint is called the vertex.
    • Angles describe how much one line turns away from another.
    🧭 How to Measure an Angle
    1
    Align the center
    Place the protractor's center exactly on the vertex.
    2
    Set the baseline
    Line up the zero mark with one arm of the angle.
    3
    Read the scale
    Find where the second arm meets the degree markings.
    🎯 Important Angle Targets
    • 45° - an acute angle with a sharp turn
    • 90° - a right angle, like the corner of a square
    • 120° - an obtuse angle with a wider opening
    Check the size before reading the number

    If the angle looks smaller than a right angle, it should be less than 90°.

    If it opens wider than a right angle, choose the scale that gives a value greater than 90°.

    3 6.2 Regular Polygons

    Perfect Symmetry

    🔷 What Makes a Polygon Regular
    • A regular polygon is a 2D shape with all sides equal.
    • Its interior angles are also equal.
    • Regular polygons are known for their symmetry and balanced appearance.
    👥 Polygon Family from 3 to 8 Sides
    Sides Name Everyday Clue
    3 Equilateral Triangle Three equal edges
    4 Square Four equal sides and right angles
    5 Regular Pentagon Five equal sides
    6 Regular Hexagon Honeycomb pattern
    7 Regular Heptagon Seven equal sides
    8 Regular Octagon Stop sign shape
    ✏ ️ Tools for Accurate Drawing
    • Square grids help when drawing shapes with right angles.
    • Isometric grids help show shapes from different viewpoints.
    • Computer software makes precise geometric construction easier.
    4 6.2.2 Interior Angles

    Measuring the Inside Turn

    🧮 Formula for Interior Angles
    • Use this formula to find the interior angle of a regular polygon:
    Prompt Interior angle formula
    (n - 2) x 180° / n
    • n represents the number of sides.
    📋 Interior Angle Cheat Sheet
    • Triangle: 60°
    • Square: 90°
    • Pentagon: 108°
    • Hexagon: 120°
    • Octagon: 135°
    The number of sides changes the angle pattern

    As the number of sides increases, each interior angle becomes larger and the shape looks closer to a circle.

    5 6.3 Problem Solving

    Space in the Real World

    🕵 ️ Angle Facts to Remember
    • Angles on a straight line add up to 180°.
    • Angles around a point add up to 360°.
    • These facts help us find missing angles in diagrams.
    📏 Perimeter and Area
    • Perimeter is the total distance around a shape.
    • Area is the amount of space inside a shape.
    • For regular polygons, area can sometimes be found by splitting the shape into smaller triangles.
    📦 Volume of 3D Shapes
    • Volume measures how much space a solid shape occupies.
    • For cubes and cuboids, multiply:
    Prompt Volume formula
    Length x Width x Height
    • Example: A box with length 5 cm, width 2 cm, and height 10 cm has a volume of 100 cm^3.
    6 Summary

    Checklist and Key Takeaways

    ✅ What You Should Be Able to Do
    • Use a protractor to draw 90° and 120° angles.
    • Name regular polygons from triangle to octagon.
    • Identify that a regular hexagon has an interior angle of 120°.
    • Apply the volume formula for cubes and cuboids.
    🚀

    Geometry in Daily Life

    Shapes, angles, and solids help us describe the space around us.

    📐

    Angle Skills

    Use a protractor carefully and recognize common angle sizes.

    🔷

    Regular Polygons

    Equal sides and equal angles define regular polygons from triangle to octagon.

    📦

    Problem Solving

    Use angle facts, perimeter, area, and volume to solve real-world questions.

    “
    When students can measure, name, and reason about shapes with confidence, geometry becomes a language for understanding the world.

    Keep looking for angles, polygons, and solids in everyday life.
